346	\begin{lemma}\leanok
347	[Graded Hilbert--Serre: rationality of the Hilbert series]
348	  \label{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}
349	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational}
350	  \uses{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib,
351	    def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero, lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq,
352	    lem:ratHilb_bump, def:graded_subquotientHilb,
353	    lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}
354	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
355	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
356	  % 00K1) (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13893-L13905).
357	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
358	  % "Suppose that $S$ is a Noetherian graded ring
359	  %  and $M$ a finite graded $S$-module. Consider the
360	  %  function
361	  %  $$
362	  %  \mathbf{Z} \longrightarrow K'_0(S_0), \quad
363	  %  n \longmapsto [M_n]
364	  %  $$
365	  %  see Lemma \ref{lemma-graded-module-fg}.
366	  %  If $S_{+}$ is generated by elements of degree $1$,
367	  %  then this function is a numerical polynomial."
368	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", Tag 00K1
369	  (proposition on graded Hilbert polynomials); cf.\ [Nitsure], \S 1 (Snapper's
370	  Lemma), [Hartshorne], I.7.}
371	  Let \(R\) be a Noetherian graded ring whose degree-zero part is a field
372	  \(\kappa\) and which is generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra by finitely many
373	  elements, and let \(M\) be a finitely generated graded \(R\)-module with
374	  finite-dimensional graded components \(M_n\). Then there exist
375	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\)
376	  such that for all \(n > N\)
377	  \[
378	    \dim_{\kappa} M_n \;=\; \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr);
379	  \]
380	  that is, the Hilbert series \(\sum_{n \ge 0} (\dim_\kappa M_n)\, X^n\) is, up to
381	  a polynomial correction in low degrees, the rational function
382	  \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\), expressed in the power-series form
383	  \(\operatorname{toPowerSeries}(p) \cdot \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, d)\)
384	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}).
385	
386	  \emph{Status.} This is the substantive half of the graded Hilbert--Serre
387	  theorem (rationality of the Hilbert series of a finitely generated graded
388	  module). It is not covered by Mathlib: \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
389	  supplies only the converse polynomial-extraction step from a rational series, not
390	  the production of the rational series from the module. The argument is realised by
391	  an \emph{ambient subquotient induction} (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}):
392	  the whole induction ranges over pairs of homogeneous submodules \(N' \le N\) of the
393	  single fixed graded module \(M\), and the Hilbert function is the ambient difference
394	  \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap M_n) - \dim_\kappa(N' \cap M_n)\). The class of such
395	  pairs is closed under the kernel and cokernel of multiplication by a degree-one
396	  element (each presented again as an ambient subquotient pair), so the inductive step
397	  never forms a graded quotient ring \(R/(x)\), a graded quotient module \(M/xM\), or a
398	  regrading of a derived carrier. The genuine Mathlib gap is thereby \emph{avoided}
399	  rather than filled; the residual obligation is the short ambient bookkeeping of the
400	  subquotient blocks (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb},
401	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker},
402	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff},
403	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer},
404	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}), each of which manipulates only ambient
405	  families \(n \mapsto N_{\mathrm{aux}} \cap M_n\) of submodules of \(M\).
406	  % NOTE: The power-series rationality toolkit (\cref{subsec:isRatHilb}) is formalized
407	  %   and axiom-clean. As of iter-020 the present theorem IS a closed, axiom-clean Lean
408	  %   declaration (gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational); the ambient subquotient route
409	  %   (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}) sidesteps missing graded-quotient API by
410	  %   working with ambient families of submodules of \(M\).
411	\end{lemma}
412	
413	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, inductive step (read
414	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13938-L13947).
415	% "Let $\overline{M} = M/xM$. Note that the map $x : M \to M$
416	%  is {\it not} a map of graded $S$-modules, since it does
417	%  not map $M_d$ into $M_d$. Namely, for each $d$ we have the
418	%  following short exact sequence
419	%  $$
420	%  0 \to M_d \xrightarrow{x} M_{d + 1} \to \overline{M}_{d + 1} \to 0
421	%  $$
422	%  This proves that $[M_{d + 1}] - [M_d] = [\overline{M}_{d + 1}]$.
423	%  Hence we win by Lemma \ref{lemma-numerical-polynomial}."
424	\begin{proof}
425	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero, lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq,
426	    lem:ratHilb_bump, lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff,
427	    def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
428	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
429	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb,
430	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
431	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
432	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib,
433	    lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
434	    \leanok
435	  We argue with the abstract graded \(\kappa\)-algebra
436	  \(R = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} R_n\), whose degree-zero part \(R_0 = \kappa\) is a
437	  field, and the finitely generated graded \(R\)-module
438	  \(M = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} M_n\) with finite-dimensional components \(M_n\);
439	  there is no appeal to any geometric realisation of \(R\) or \(M\).
440	
441	  \emph{Reduction to generation in degree \(1\).} Since \(R\) is Noetherian and
442	  finitely generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra, it is generated by finitely many
443	  homogeneous elements of positive degree. Replacing \(R\) by a Veronese
444	  subalgebra \(R^{(e)} = \bigoplus_n R_{en}\) for a suitable \(e\) and re-grading
445	  (and \(M\) by the corresponding Veronese module), we may assume \(R\) is
446	  generated by its degree-one part, \(\kappa[R_1] = R\). The degree-one part
447	  \(R_1\) is then a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space whose \(\kappa\)-basis
448	  \(x_1, \dots, x_r\) generates \(R\) as a \(\kappa\)-algebra; \(r = \dim_\kappa R_1\)
449	  is the number on which we induct.
450	  % NOTE: The existence of finitely many degree-one generators (the number r the
451	  % induction is on) is expected to follow from the standing hypotheses --
452	  % "finitely generated κ-algebra" together with "generated in degree 1" -- so no
453	  % new hypothesis on gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational is anticipated. To be
454	  % confirmed when the prover formalizes the assembly: degree-1 generation gives
455	  % R₁ as a finite-dimensional κ-vector space spanning R as a κ-algebra, and a
456	  % κ-basis of R₁ is a finite degree-one generating set.
457	
458	  \emph{Set-up of the ambient data.} Regard \(M\) as the fixed ambient graded
459	  \(\kappa\)-module with internal decomposition \(n \mapsto \mathcal{M}_n := M_n\)
460	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}); the components are finite-dimensional. For each
461	  basis vector \(x_i \in R_1\) let \(t_i : M \to M\) be multiplication by \(x_i\), a
462	  \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism that raises degree by one (it sends \(M_n\) into
463	  \(M_{n+1}\), \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}). Since \(R\) is
464	  commutative the \(t_i\) pairwise commute, and they trivially preserve the pair of
465	  homogeneous submodules \((N, N') = (\top, \bot)\). Because \(R\) is generated by
466	  \(x_1, \dots, x_r\) and \(M\) is finitely generated over \(R\), \(M\) is finitely
467	  generated as a module over the polynomial ring \(\kappa[x_1, \dots, x_r]\) acting
468	  through the \(t_i\); this is the finiteness condition required below.
469	
470	  \emph{Invoking the ambient subquotient induction.} Apply
471	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb} to the pair \((N, N') = (\top, \bot)\)
472	  with the \(r\) commuting degree-\((+1)\) endomorphisms \(t_1, \dots, t_r\). It
473	  yields \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) of order \(r\) for the ambient subquotient Hilbert
474	  function of this pair (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}),
475	  \[
476	    h(n) \;=\; \dim_\kappa(\top \cap \mathcal{M}_n) - \dim_\kappa(\bot \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
477	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa M_n - 0 \;=\; \dim_\kappa M_n .
478	  \]
479	  Hence \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa M_n,\, r)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}):
480	  there are \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\), \(d := r \in \mathbb{N}\) and \(N \in \mathbb{N}\)
481	  with
482	  \[
483	    \dim_\kappa M_n \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr)
484	    \qquad (n > N),
485	  \]
486	  which is the claim, with pole order \(d = r\). The internal mechanics of
487	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb} -- the base case via
488	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}, and the inductive step assembling the
489	  kernel/cokernel closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}), the degreewise
490	  difference identity (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}, built on
491	  \(D_5\), \cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}) and the finiteness transfer
492	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}) into
493	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq} and \cref{lem:ratHilb_bump} -- are the Stacks~00K1
494	  induction realised entirely with ambient homogeneous submodules of \(M\).
495	\end{proof}
496	
497	\subsection{The rationality toolkit \texorpdfstring{\(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\)}{IsRatHilb}}
498	\label{subsec:isRatHilb}
499	% NOTE: Declarations in this subsection are currently \texttt{private} in the Lean
500	% source; \lean{} pins may not resolve until they are moved to a dedicated module.
501	
502	The proof of \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} runs the Stacks~00K1 induction
503	on the number of degree-one generators. Each inductive step manipulates the
504	\emph{shape} of the Hilbert series -- a numerator polynomial over a power of
505	\((1-X)\) -- rather than the module itself. We isolate that bookkeeping in a
506	single predicate \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) on functions \(f : \mathbb{N} \to
507	\mathbb{Q}\), together with the closure lemmas the induction consumes. These are
508	project-internal helpers realising standard steps of the Stacks~00K1 argument at
509	the level of power series; Mathlib supplies only the converse extraction
510	(\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}).
511	
512	\begin{lemma}\leanok
513	[Coefficients of \((1-X)^{-1}\) times a series are partial sums]
514	  \label{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul}
515	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul}
516	  \uses{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
517	  Let \(F \in \mathbb{Q}\llbracket X\rrbracket\) be a power series. Then for every
518	  \(n \in \mathbb{N}\) the \(n\)-th coefficient of
519	  \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, 1)\cdot F = (1-X)^{-1}\cdot F\)
520	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}) is the partial sum of the coefficients of
521	  \(F\):
522	  \[
523	    \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl((1-X)^{-1}\cdot F\bigr)
524	    \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{n} \bigl[X^k\bigr] F .
525	  \]
526	  Equivalently, multiplication by \((1-X)^{-1} = \sum_{m\ge 0} X^m\) is the
527	  summation operator on coefficient sequences. Proved directly in Lean from the
528	  identity \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q},1) = \sum_{m\ge 0} X^m\) and
529	  the Cauchy product.
530	\end{lemma}
531	
532	\begin{lemma}\leanok
533	[Antidifference of a rational Hilbert series]
534	  \label{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
535	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rationalHilbert_antidiff}
536	  \uses{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
537	  Let \(H, \delta : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\), let \(q \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and
538	  \(e, N \in \mathbb{N}\). Suppose that for all \(n > N\) the sequence \(\delta\)
539	  is the coefficient sequence of the rational series \(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\),
540	  \[
541	    \delta(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\bigr),
542	  \]
543	  and that \(\delta\) is the first difference of \(H\) beyond \(N\), i.e.
544	  \(H(n+1) - H(n) = \delta(n+1)\) for all \(n > N\). Then there is a numerator
545	  polynomial \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) such that for all \(n > N\)
546	  \[
547	    H(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr).
548	  \]
549	  That is, summing a coefficient sequence (anti-differencing) raises the pole
550	  order by one: the partial-sum series of \(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\) equals
551	  \(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\) up to a polynomial correction in low degrees. This is
552	  the power-series heart of the Stacks~00K1 inductive step.
553	\end{lemma}
554	
555	\begin{proof}
556	  \uses{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
557	  \leanok
558	  Write \(F = q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\). Multiplying by
559	  \((1-X)^{-1}\) and using the factorisation
560	  \((1-X)^{-(e+1)} = (1-X)^{-1}\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\)
561	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}), \cref{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul} gives
562	  \(\bigl[X^m\bigr]\bigl(q\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr) = \sum_{k=0}^{m}[X^k]F\), the
563	  partial sums of \(F\). On the other hand, telescoping the first-difference
564	  hypothesis from \(N+1\) expresses \(H(N+1+j)\) as \(H(N+1)\) plus a window sum
565	  of the coefficients of \(F\). Splitting the partial sum
566	  \(\sum_{k=0}^{n}[X^k]F\) at \(N+2\) and absorbing the resulting constant
567	  discrepancy into a numerator \(C\,(1-X)^{e}\) (a constant function is the
568	  order-\((e+1)\) coefficient sequence of \(C\,(1-X)^{e}\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)} =
569	  C\,(1-X)^{-1}\)), the explicit numerator
570	  \(p = C\,(1-X)^{e} + q\) realises \(H(n)\) as
571	  \([X^n]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr)\) for all \(n > N\).
572	\end{proof}
573	
574	\begin{definition}\leanok
575	[Rational Hilbert function]
576	  \label{def:ratHilb}
577	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb}
578	  \uses{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
579	  \textit{Source: Stacks 00K1.}
580	  For a function \(f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\), say
581	  that \(f\) is a \emph{rational Hilbert function of order \(d\)}, written
582	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\), when there exist a numerator polynomial
583	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) such that for all \(n > N\)
584	  \[
585	    f(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-d}\bigr)
586	    \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(\operatorname{toPowerSeries}(p)\cdot
587	      \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, d)\bigr)
588	  \]
589	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}). This is the ``eventually equal to the
590	  coefficient sequence of a rational series \(p\cdot(1-X)^{-d}\)'' predicate that
591	  the graded Hilbert--Serre induction tracks; its conclusion shape is exactly the
592	  one consumed by \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}.
593	\end{definition}
594	
595	\begin{lemma}\leanok
596	[Base case: eventually-zero functions are rational of order \(0\)]
597	  \label{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}
598	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.ofEventuallyZero}
599	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
600	  If \(f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) is eventually zero -- there is
601	  \(N\) with \(f(n) = 0\) for all \(n > N\) -- then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, 0)\)
602	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}). Indeed \(f\) is then the coefficient sequence of the
603	  numerator polynomial \(0\) over \((1-X)^{0} = 1\). This is the base case of the
604	  Stacks~00K1 induction (no degree-one generators). Proved directly in Lean.
605	\end{lemma}
606	
607	\begin{lemma}\leanok
608	[Raising the pole order]
609	  \label{lem:ratHilb_bump}
610	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.bump}
611	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
612	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) then
613	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d+1)\): multiplying the numerator by \((1-X)\) cancels
614	  one factor of \((1-X)^{-1}\), so the same coefficient sequence is realised at
615	  pole order \(d+1\). This lets two rational Hilbert functions of different
616	  orders be brought to a common denominator. Proved directly in Lean using
617	  \((1-X)\cdot(1-X)^{-(d+1)} = (1-X)^{-d}\) (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}).
618	\end{lemma}
619	
620	\begin{lemma}\leanok
621	[Closure under difference]
622	  \label{lem:ratHilb_sub}
623	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.sub}
624	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
625	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) and \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, d)\)
626	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}) at the same order \(d\), then
627	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto f(n) - g(n),\, d)\): the numerator of the
628	  difference is the difference of the numerators (over the common denominator
629	  \((1-X)^{-d}\)). Proved directly in Lean.
630	\end{lemma}
631	
632	\begin{lemma}\leanok
633	[Closure under right shift]
634	  \label{lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
635	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.shiftRight}
636	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
637	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) then
638	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto f(n-1),\, d)\): the right shift on coefficient
639	  sequences is multiplication of the series by \(X\), so the numerator
640	  \(p\) is replaced by \(X\cdot p\) at the same pole order. Proved directly in
641	  Lean.
642	\end{lemma}
643	
644	\begin{lemma}\leanok
645	[Antidifference step for the predicate]
646	  \label{lem:ratHilb_antidiff}
647	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.antidiff}
648	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
649	  Let \(H, g : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(e, N \in \mathbb{N}\). If
650	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, e)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) and \(g\) is the first
651	  difference of \(H\) beyond \(N\) (\(H(n+1) - H(n) = g(n+1)\) for all
652	  \(n > N\)), then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(H, e+1)\). This is the predicate-level
653	  packaging of \cref{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}: a finite difference raises
654	  the pole order by one, the inductive heart of graded Hilbert--Serre.
655	\end{lemma}
656	
657	\begin{proof}
658	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
659	  \leanok
660	  Unfolding \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, e)\) supplies a numerator \(q\) and a
661	  threshold \(N_g\) with \(g(n) = [X^n](q\cdot(1-X)^{-e})\) for \(n > N_g\).
662	  Applying \cref{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff} with this \(q\) and threshold
663	  \(\max(N, N_g)\) -- whose two hypotheses are exactly the rational form of \(g\)
664	  and the first-difference identity for \(H\) -- yields a numerator \(p\) with
665	  \(H(n) = [X^n](p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)})\) for \(n > \max(N, N_g)\), i.e.
666	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(H, e+1)\).
667	\end{proof}
668	
669	\begin{lemma}\leanok
670	[Inductive-step engine from a degreewise short exact sequence]
671	  \label{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}
672	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.ofDiffEq}
673	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_antidiff, lem:ratHilb_sub, lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
674	  Let \(h_M, h_C, h_K : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(d, N \in \mathbb{N}\).
675	  Suppose \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, d)\) and \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, d)\)
676	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}), and that for all \(n > N\)
677	  \[
678	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
679	  \]
680	  Then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_M, d+1)\). This packages the entire power-series
681	  side of the Stacks~00K1 inductive step: \(h_M\) is the Hilbert function of
682	  \(M\), and \(h_C, h_K\) are the Hilbert functions of the cokernel
683	  \(C = M/xM\) and kernel \(K = \ker(x : M \to M(1))\) of multiplication by a
684	  degree-one element \(x\); the displayed identity is the alternating-sum
685	  consequence of the degreewise short exact sequence
686	  \(0 \to K_n \to M_n \xrightarrow{x} M_{n+1} \to C_{n+1} \to 0\). Given the
687	  inductive hypothesis that \(h_C, h_K\) are rational of order \(d\), the
688	  conclusion is rationality of \(h_M\) at order \(d+1\).
689	\end{lemma}
690	
691	\begin{proof}
692	  \uses{lem:ratHilb_antidiff, lem:ratHilb_sub, lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
693	  \leanok
694	  Set \(g(n) = h_C(n) - h_K(n-1)\). By closure under right shift
695	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}) \(n \mapsto h_K(n-1)\) is rational of order
696	  \(d\), and by closure under difference (\cref{lem:ratHilb_sub}) so is \(g\). The
697	  hypothesis rewrites as \(h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) = g(n+1)\) for \(n > N\) (since
698	  \(g(n+1) = h_C(n+1) - h_K(n)\)). Applying the antidifference step
699	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_antidiff}) to \(H = h_M\) and this \(g\) gives
700	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_M, d+1)\).
701	\end{proof}

703	\begin{theorem}[Hilbert polynomial from the section module]
704	  \label{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
705	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomialOfSectionModule}
706	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
707	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
708	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
709	  % Polynomials" (read from
710	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L478).
711	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
712	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
713	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
714	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
715	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$. Then for each $s\in S$,
716	  %  we get a polynomial ${\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]$
717	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
718	  %  the restriction $\F_s = F|_{X_s}$ of $\F$ to the fiber $X_s$ over $s$,
719	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle $L_s = L|_{X_s}$."
720	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
721	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
722	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
723	  Then there is a unique polynomial \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
724	  such that for all \(m \gg 0\)
725	  \[
726	    \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)
727	    \;=\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr).
728	  \]
729	  This \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is the Hilbert polynomial of
730	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}.
731	\end{theorem}
732	
733	\begin{proof}
734	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
735	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
736	  By \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg} the section graded module
737	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded module over the
738	  Noetherian graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\), with finite-dimensional components and
739	  degree-zero part the field \(\kappa(s)\). Applying the abstract graded
740	  Hilbert--Serre rationality lemma (\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) to the
741	  graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra \(R = R(X_s, L_s)\) and the finitely generated
742	  graded module \(M = M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) exhibits the graded Hilbert
743	  function
744	  \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
745	  as the eventual coefficient function of a rational series: there are
746	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N\) with
747	  \(\dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
748	  = [X^m]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\) for \(m > N\). Apply
749	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib} over the characteristic-zero field
750	  \(F = \mathbb{Q}\) to this pair \((p, d)\): it yields a unique polynomial
751	  \(h \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) whose values \(h(n)\) eventually equal
752	  \([X^n]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\). Setting
753	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} := h\) (with the variable renamed \(\lambda\)) gives a
754	  polynomial with \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m) = \dim_{\kappa(s)}
755	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) for \(m \gg 0\). Uniqueness
756	  is the \(\exists!\) clause of \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}: any two
757	  polynomials agreeing with the Hilbert function for \(m \gg 0\) agree at
758	  infinitely many points, hence are equal.
759	\end{proof}

761	\subsection{The ambient subquotient induction for the Stacks~00K1 step}
762	\label{subsec:gradedModuleApi}
763	
764	The power-series side of \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} is fully isolated
765	in the \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) toolkit (\cref{subsec:isRatHilb}). What remains is
766	the \emph{graded-module} side of the Stacks~00K1 induction. Rather than build the
767	kernel and cokernel of multiplication by a degree-one element as graded modules
768	over a smaller quotient ring \(R/(x)\) -- which would force a graded structure on a
769	derived carrier -- we range the induction over \emph{pairs of homogeneous
770	submodules of a single fixed ambient graded module} \(M\). A pair \((N, N')\) with
771	\(N' \le N\) homogeneous submodules of \(M\) stands for the subquotient \(N/N'\); its
772	ambient Hilbert function is the difference \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap M_n) -
773	\dim_\kappa(N' \cap M_n)\). The class of such pairs, equipped with finitely many
774	pairwise-commuting degree-one endomorphisms preserving \(N\) and \(N'\), is
775	\emph{closed} under the kernel and cokernel of one of those endomorphisms, both
776	again presented as ambient subquotient pairs. Consequently no graded quotient ring,
777	graded quotient module, or regrading of a subtype/quotient carrier is ever formed:
778	every object that appears is an ambient family \(n \mapsto N_{\mathrm{aux}} \cap M_n\)
779	of submodules of the fixed \(M\).
780	
781	This subsection records that machinery as a thin layer over existing Mathlib
782	scaffold: Mathlib already provides the notion of a homogeneous submodule
783	(\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}), the homogeneity of a span of
784	homogeneous elements (\cref{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib}), the degree-shift
785	of multiplication by a homogeneous element
786	(\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}), the rank--nullity identity
787	(\cref{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}), and the finite-generation
788	transfer along a surjection (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}).
789	What is supplied here is the internal direct-sum decomposition of a homogeneous
790	submodule as an ambient family (the split \(G_1\)), the subquotient Hilbert data,
791	the kernel/cokernel closure, the degreewise difference identity, the ambient
792	finiteness transfer, and the induction itself.
793	
794	\emph{Setting for this subsection.} Let \(R = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} R_n\) be a graded
795	ring whose degree-zero part \(R_0 = \kappa\) is a field and which is generated as a
796	\(\kappa\)-algebra in degree one, and let \(M = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} M_n\) be a graded
797	\(\kappa\)-vector space (the fixed ambient module) with finite-dimensional
798	components \(M_n\). All submodules \(N, N', \dots\) below are homogeneous submodules
799	of \(M\), and the relevant endomorphisms are \(\kappa\)-linear maps \(M \to M\)
800	raising degree by one -- typically multiplication by a homogeneous element
801	\(x \in R_1\).
802	
803	\subsubsection*{Mathlib dependency anchors}
804	
805	\begin{lemma}[Homogeneous submodule]
806	  \label{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
807	  \lean{Submodule.IsHomogeneous}
808	  \mathlibok
809	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
810	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.GradedAlgebra.Homogeneous.Submodule}).}
811	  Let \(R = \bigoplus_n R_n\) be a graded ring and \(M = \bigoplus_n M_n\) an
812	  internally graded \(R\)-module. A submodule \(p \subseteq M\) is
813	  \emph{homogeneous} when it is closed under taking homogeneous components:
814	  \(m \in p\) implies each graded piece \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in p\). Equivalently
815	  \(p = \bigoplus_n (p \cap M_n)\) as a set. Mathlib bundles this into a homogeneous
816	  submodule type providing order and extensionality structure; the ambient internal
817	  direct-sum decomposition \(p = \bigoplus_n (M_n \cap p)\) is supplied by the split
818	  \(G_1\) (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep},
819	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}).
820	\end{lemma}
821	
822	\begin{lemma}[A span of homogeneous elements is homogeneous]
823	  \label{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib}
824	  \lean{Ideal.homogeneous_span}
825	  \mathlibok
826	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
827	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.GradedAlgebra.Homogeneous.Ideal}).}
828	  Let \(R = \bigoplus_n R_n\) be a graded ring and \(s \subseteq R\) a set each of
829	  whose elements is homogeneous. Then the ideal \(\mathrm{span}\,s\) is a
830	  homogeneous ideal. In particular, for \(x \in R_1\) the principal ideal \((x)\)
831	  is homogeneous, hence \(xM\) and the auxiliary submodule \(N' + x \cdot N\) are
832	  homogeneous; this homogeneity feeds the kernel/cokernel closure
833	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}).
834	\end{lemma}
835	
836	\begin{lemma}[Rank--nullity]
837	  \label{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}
838	  \lean{LinearMap.finrank_range_add_finrank_ker}
839	  \mathlibok
840	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
841	  (\texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.FiniteDimensional.Lemmas}).}
842	  Let \(\kappa\) be a field, \(V\) a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space and
843	  \(\varphi : V \to W\) a \(\kappa\)-linear map. Then
844	  \[
845	    \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) + \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)
846	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa V .
847	  \]
848	  This is the rank--nullity identity underlying the degreewise difference identity
849	  \(D_5\) (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}).
850	\end{lemma}

852	\begin{lemma}[Finite generation transfers along a surjection]
853	  \label{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
854	  \lean{Submodule.FG.restrictScalars_of_surjective}
855	  \mathlibok
856	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Finiteness.Basic}).}

858	  \(A\)-module that is finitely generated when viewed as an \(R\)-module via
859	  \(\varphi\). Then \(M\) is finitely generated as an \(A\)-module. Applied to the
860	  surjection \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \twoheadrightarrow \kappa[t_0, \dots,
861	  t_{r-2}]\) killing the endomorphism \(x = t_{r-1}\) that annihilates the two

865	
866	\begin{lemma}[Multiplication by a homogeneous element shifts degree]
867	  \label{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}
868	  \lean{SetLike.IsHomogeneousElem.graded_smul}
869	  \mathlibok
870	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.GradedMulAction}).}
871	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be a graded family acting on a graded family \(\mathcal{M}\)

873	  \(i\) -- in particular \(x \in R_1\) -- then for \(m \in M_n\) one has

882	  \label{lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib}

1	\chapter{The Grassmannian over $\mathbb{Z}$: affine charts and gluing}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianCells}
3	
4	\section{Set-up: charts indexed by $d$-subsets}
5	\label{sec:gr_setup}
6	
7	Fix integers \(r \ge d \ge 1\). For a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M\) over a ring
8	and a subset \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\), write \(M_I\)
9	for the \emph{\(I\)-th minor} of \(M\): the \(d \times d\) submatrix whose
10	columns are those indexed by \(I\). We construct the Grassmannian scheme
11	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing one affine chart \(U^I\) for
12	each such \(I\). The construction is, conceptually, the quotient
13	\(\mathrm{GL}_{d,\mathbb{Z}} \backslash V\) of the scheme \(V\) of rank-\(d\)
14	matrices, but we never use the language of group-scheme actions: the charts and
15	their transition maps are elementary matrix algebra over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Taking
16	\(d = 1\) recovers \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,1) = \mathbb{P}^{r-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\).
17	
18	\begin{definition}
19	
20	\leanok
21	[Affine chart \(U^I\)]
22	  \label{def:gr_affine_chart}
23	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.affineChart}
24	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
25	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
26	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L807-L821).
27	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
28	  % "If $M$ is a $d\times r$-matrix, and
29	  %  $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with cardinality $\#(I)$ equal
30	  %  to $d$, the $I$ th minor $M_I$ of $M$ will
31	  %  mean the $d\times d$ minor of $M$ whose columns are indexed by
32	  %  $I$.
33	  %
34	  %  For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$,
35	  %  consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
36	  %  $X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
37	  %  entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$.
38	  %  Let $\Z[X^I]$ denote the polynomial ring in the variables
39	  %  $x^I_{p,q}$, and let $U^I = \spec \Z[X^I]$, which is non-canonically
40	  %  isomorphic to the affine space $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$."
41	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
42	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). Let \(X^I\) denote the
43	  \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) is the \(d \times d\)
44	  identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\) and whose remaining \(d(r - d)\) entries are
45	  independent indeterminates \(x^I_{p,q}\) (\(1 \le p \le d\),
46	  \(q \in \{1,\dots,r\} \setminus I\)) over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Write
47	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) for the polynomial ring in these \(d(r-d)\) variables and
48	  set
49	  \[
50	    U^I \;:=\; \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I].
51	  \]
52	  The choice of an ordering of the free entries gives a (non-canonical)
53	  isomorphism \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\); in particular
54	  \(U^I\) is a smooth affine \(\mathbb{Z}\)-scheme of relative dimension
55	  \(d(r-d)\). Intuitively a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-point of \(U^I\) is a \(d \times r\)
56	  matrix whose \(I\)-columns form the identity, i.e.\ a rank-\(d\) quotient of
57	  \(\mathbb{Z}^{r}\) trivialised on the coordinates \(I\).
58	\end{definition}
59	
60	\section{Transition maps and the cocycle condition}
61	\label{sec:gr_transition}
62	
63	\subsection*{Mathlib inputs for the transition map}
64	
65	The construction of \(\theta_{I,J}\) is a Cramer-inverse computation over the
66	localised chart ring, followed by one application of the universal property of a
67	localisation. We record the four Mathlib facts it rests on as dependency
68	anchors.
69	
70	\begin{lemma}
71	[Localised element is a unit]
72	  \label{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
73	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.algebraMap_isUnit}
74	  \mathlibok
75	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
76	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(x \in R\), and \(S\) a localisation of \(R\)
77	  away from \(x\) (i.e.\ \(S = R[1/x]\)). Then the image of \(x\) under the
78	  structure map \(R \to S\) is a unit of \(S\).
79	\end{lemma}
80	
81	\begin{lemma}
82	[Left Cramer inverse]
83	  \label{lem:mathlib_nonsing_inv_mul}
84	  \lean{Matrix.nonsing_inv_mul}
85	  \mathlibok
86	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
87	  For a square matrix \(A\) over a commutative ring with \(\det A\) a unit, the
88	  nonsingular inverse \(A^{-1}\) satisfies \(A^{-1} A = 1\).
89	\end{lemma}
90	
91	\begin{lemma}
92	[Right Cramer inverse]
93	  \label{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
94	  \lean{Matrix.mul_nonsing_inv}
95	  \mathlibok
96	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
97	  For a square matrix \(A\) over a commutative ring with \(\det A\) a unit, the
98	  nonsingular inverse \(A^{-1}\) satisfies \(A A^{-1} = 1\).
99	\end{lemma}
100	
101	\begin{lemma}
102	[Universal property of an away-localisation]
103	  \label{lem:mathlib_away_lift}
104	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.lift}
105	  \mathlibok
106	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
107	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(x \in R\), and \(S = R[1/x]\) a localisation
108	  of \(R\) away from \(x\). Given any ring homomorphism \(g : R \to P\) that
109	  sends \(x\) to a unit of \(P\), there is a unique ring homomorphism
110	  \(S \to P\) extending \(g\) along \(R \to S\).
111	\end{lemma}
112	
113	\subsection*{The transition map by matrix algebra}
114	
115	We now build \(\theta_{I,J}\) in seven steps over the localised chart ring
116	\(R^I_J := \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\), splitting at the matrix-algebra seams. The
117	ambient chart ring \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) is the polynomial ring of
118	\cref{def:gr_affine_chart} in the \(d(r-d)\) free variables \(x^I_{p,q}\)
119	(\(p \in \{1,\dots,d\}\), \(q \notin I\)).
120	
121	\begin{definition}
122	
123	\leanok
124	[The universal matrix \(X^I\)]
125	  \label{def:gr_universal_matrix}
126	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMatrix}
127	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart}
128	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
129	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
130	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L807-L821).
131	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
132	  % "For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$,
133	  %  consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
134	  %  $X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
135	  %  entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$."
136	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). With the chart ring
137	  %   `R^I := MvPolynomial (Fin d × {q : Fin r // q ∉ I}) ℤ`, define
138	  %   `universalMatrix d r I : Matrix (Fin d) (Fin r) R^I` whose entry at
139	  %   `(p, q)` is: the identity entry `if (orderIso I) p = q then 1 else 0`
140	  %   when `q ∈ I` (using the order isomorphism `Fin d ≃o I` from `#I = d`),
141	  %   and the indeterminate `MvPolynomial.X (p, ⟨q, hq⟩)` when `q ∉ I`.
142	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
143	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). The \emph{universal
144	  matrix} \(X^I\) is the \(d \times r\) matrix over the chart ring
145	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) whose \(I\)-minor \(X^I_I\) is the \(d \times d\) identity
146	  \(1_{d\times d}\) and whose remaining entries are the free indeterminates
147	  \(x^I_{p,q}\) (\(1 \le p \le d\), \(q \notin I\)). It is the universal point of
148	  the chart \(U^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}): a ring map
149	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to A\) is the same as a \(d \times r\) matrix over \(A\)
150	  with identity \(I\)-minor.
151	\end{definition}
152	
153	\begin{definition}
154	
155	\leanok
156	[The minor determinant \(P^I_J\)]
157	  \label{def:gr_minor_det}
158	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.minorDet}
159	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix}
160	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
161	  % affine patches" (read from
162	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
163	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
164	  % "For any $J\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(J) =d$, let
165	  %  $P^I_J = \det(X^I_J) \in \Z[X^I]$
166	  %  where $X^I_J$ is the $J$ th minor of $X^I$."
167	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). `minorDet d r I J : R^I` is the
168	  %   determinant of the `d × d` submatrix of `universalMatrix d r I` whose
169	  %   columns are indexed by `J`, reindexed to `Fin d` via the order
170	  %   isomorphism `Fin d ≃o J` coming from `#J = d`:
171	  %   `(universalMatrix d r I).submatrix id (orderIso J) |>.det`.
172	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
173	  For \(J \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(J) = d\), let \(X^I_J\) denote the
174	  \(J\)-th minor of the universal matrix \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix})
175	  -- the \(d \times d\) submatrix whose columns are those indexed by \(J\). Set
176	  \[
177	    P^I_J \;:=\; \det\bigl(X^I_J\bigr) \;\in\; \mathbb{Z}[X^I].
178	  \]
179	  Then \(R^I_J := \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\) (the localisation of the chart ring
180	  away from \(P^I_J\)) is the ring of the principal open subscheme
181	  \(U^I_J = \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J] \subseteq U^I\) on which \(P^I_J\) is
182	  invertible. One has \(P^I_I = \det(1_{d\times d}) = 1\), so \(R^I_I =
183	  \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) and \(U^I_I = U^I\).
184	\end{definition}
185	
186	\begin{definition}
187	
188	\leanok
189	[The localised \(J\)-minor \(X^I_J\) over \(R^I_J\)]
190	  \label{def:gr_universal_minor}
191	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMinor}
192	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_minor_det}
193	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
194	  % affine patches" (read from
195	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
196	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
197	  % "Let $U^I_J = \spec \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$
198	  %  the open subscheme of $U^I$ where $P^I_J$ is invertible."
199	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target).
200	  %   `universalMinor d r I J : Matrix (Fin d) (Fin d) (Localization.Away (minorDet d r I J))`
201	  %   is the `J`-minor of `universalMatrix d r I` base-changed along the
202	  %   structure map `R^I → R^I_J = Localization.Away (minorDet d r I J)`:
203	  %   `((universalMatrix d r I).submatrix id (orderIso J)).map (algebraMap _ _)`.
204	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
205	  Write \(X^I_J \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times d}(R^I_J)\) for the \(J\)-minor of
206	  the universal matrix \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}) with its entries
207	  pushed forward along the structure map \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to R^I_J\) into the
208	  localised chart ring \(R^I_J = \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\)
209	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}). By construction \(\det(X^I_J)\) is the image of
210	  \(P^I_J\) in \(R^I_J\).
211	\end{definition}
212	
213	\begin{lemma}
214	
215	\leanok
216	[The localised minor determinant is a unit]
217	  \label{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}
218	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isUnit_det_universalMinor}
219	  \uses{def:gr_universal_minor, def:gr_minor_det, lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
220	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
221	  % affine patches" (read from
222	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
223	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
224	  % "the open subscheme of $U^I$ where $P^I_J$ is invertible. This means
225	  %  the $d\times d$-matrix $X^I_J$ admits an inverse $(X^I_J)^{-1}$
226	  %  on $U^I_J$."
227	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target).
228	  %   `isUnit_det_universalMinor d r I J : IsUnit (universalMinor d r I J).det`.
229	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
230	  The determinant of the localised \(J\)-minor \(X^I_J\)
231	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_minor}) is a unit of \(R^I_J\):
232	  \[
233	    \det\bigl(X^I_J\bigr) \in (R^I_J)^{\times}.
234	  \]
235	\end{lemma}
236	\begin{proof}
237	  \uses{def:gr_universal_minor, def:gr_minor_det, lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
238	  \leanok
239	  By \cref{def:gr_universal_minor}, \(\det(X^I_J)\) is the image of \(P^I_J\)
240	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}) under the structure map
241	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to R^I_J\). Since \(R^I_J\) is the localisation away from
242	  \(P^I_J\), that image is a unit by
243	  \cref{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}.
244	\end{proof}
245	
246	\begin{definition}
247	
248	\leanok
249	[The Cramer inverse \((X^I_J)^{-1}\)]
250	  \label{def:gr_universalMinorInv}
251	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMinorInv}
252	  \uses{def:gr_universal_minor}
253	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
254	  % affine patches" (read from
255	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
256	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
257	  % "the $d\times d$-matrix $X^I_J$ admits an inverse $(X^I_J)^{-1}$
258	  %  on $U^I_J$."
259	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target).
260	  %   `universalMinorInv d r I J : Matrix (Fin d) (Fin d) (Localization.Away (minorDet d r I J))`
261	  %   `:= (universalMinor d r I J)⁻¹` (the Mathlib nonsingular inverse).
262	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
263	  Let \((X^I_J)^{-1} \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times d}(R^I_J)\) be the
264	  nonsingular (Cramer) inverse of the localised \(J\)-minor \(X^I_J\)
265	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_minor}). Its entries lie in \(R^I_J\) by Cramer's rule.
266	\end{definition}
267	
268	\begin{lemma}
269	
270	\leanok
271	[The Cramer inverse is a two-sided inverse]
272	  \label{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}
273	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMinorInv_mul_cancel}
274	  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_minorDet_unit, lem:mathlib_nonsing_inv_mul, lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
275	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). A conjunction (or pair of
276	  %   lemmas):
277	  %   `universalMinorInv d r I J * universalMinor d r I J = 1` and
278	  %   `universalMinor d r I J * universalMinorInv d r I J = 1`.
279	  Because \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit of \(R^I_J\) (\cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}),
280	  the Cramer inverse \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}) is a
281	  genuine two-sided inverse:
282	  \[
283	    (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I_J = 1_{d\times d}
284	    \qquad\text{and}\qquad
285	    X^I_J\, (X^I_J)^{-1} = 1_{d\times d}.
286	  \]
287	\end{lemma}
288	\begin{proof}
289	  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_minorDet_unit, lem:mathlib_nonsing_inv_mul, lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
290	  \leanok

292	  \cref{lem:mathlib_nonsing_inv_mul} (left identity) and
293	  \cref{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv} (right identity), both of which apply once
294	  \(\det(X^I_J)\) is known to be a unit.
295	\end{proof}

297	\begin{definition}
298	
299	\leanok
300	[The image matrix \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\)]
301	  \label{def:gr_image_matrix}
302	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.imageMatrix}
303	  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, def:gr_universal_matrix}
304	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
305	  % affine patches" (read from
306	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
307	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
308	  % "The images of the variables
309	  %  $x^J_{p,q}$ are given by the entries of the matrix formula
310	  %  $\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J) ^{-1}\,X^I$."
311	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target).
312	  %   `imageMatrix d r I J : Matrix (Fin d) (Fin r) (Localization.Away (minorDet d r I J))`

318	  \]

329	
330	\leanok

332	  \label{def:gr_transition_pre}

334	  \uses{def:gr_image_matrix}

336	  % affine patches" (read from
337	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).

339	  % "a ring homomorphism
340	  %  $\theta_{I,J} : \Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \to \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$ is
341	  %  defined as follows. The images of the variables
342	  %  $x^J_{p,q}$ are given by the entries of the matrix formula
343	  %  $\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J) ^{-1}\,X^I$."
344	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). Via the universal property of the
345	  %   polynomial chart ring `R^J := MvPolynomial (Fin d × {q : Fin r // q ∉ J}) ℤ`,
346	  %   `transitionPreMap d r I J : R^J →ₐ[ℤ] Localization.Away (minorDet d r I J)`
347	  %   `:= MvPolynomial.aeval (fun e => imageMatrix d r I J e.1 e.2.1)`, i.e. the
348	  %   free indeterminate `x^J_{p,q}` is sent to the `(p,q)`-entry of `imageMatrix`.
349	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
350	  Let \(\tilde\theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to R^I_J\) be the
351	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism out of the chart ring of \(J\) determined,
352	  via the universal property of the polynomial ring, by sending each free
353	  indeterminate \(x^J_{p,q}\) to the \((p,q)\)-entry of the image matrix \(M =
354	  (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_image_matrix}); equivalently
355	  \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) as a matrix identity (the
356	  identity \(J\)-block of \(X^J\) matching the identity \(J\)-minor of \(M\)).
357	\end{definition}

359	\begin{lemma}
360	
361	\leanok
362	[The pre-hom sends \(P^J_I\) to a unit]
363	  \label{lem:gr_transition_pre_unit}
364	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isUnit_transitionPreMap_minorDet}
365	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, def:gr_minor_det, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_minorDet_unit}
366	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
367	  % affine patches" (read from
368	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
369	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
370	  % "In particular, we have $\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = 1/P^I_J$,
371	  %  so the map extends to $\Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I]$."
372	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target).
373	  %   `isUnit_transitionPreMap_minorDet d r I J :`
374	  %   `IsUnit (transitionPreMap d r I J (minorDet d r J I))`.
375	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
376	  The pre-localisation hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre})
377	  sends \(P^J_I\) (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}, the \(I\)-minor determinant of
378	  \(X^J\)) to a unit of \(R^I_J\). Indeed
379	  \[
380	    \tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I\bigr)
381	      = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr) = \det(X^I_J)^{-1} = 1/P^I_J,
382	  \]
383	  which is a unit.
384	\end{lemma}
385	\begin{proof}
386	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, def:gr_minor_det, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_minorDet_unit}
387	  \leanok
388	  By \cref{def:gr_transition_pre}, \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = M = (X^I_J)^{-1}
389	  X^I\), so \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) carries the \(I\)-minor \(X^J_I\) of \(X^J\) to
390	  the \(I\)-minor of \(M\), which is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) since
391	  the \(I\)-minor of \(X^I\) is the identity. Hence \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) =
392	  \det((X^I_J)^{-1}) = \det(X^I_J)^{-1}\). As \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit
393	  (\cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}), so is its inverse; the matrix-inverse
394	  determinant identity uses \cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}.
395	\end{proof}

397	\begin{definition}
398	
399	\leanok
400	[Transition map \(\theta_{I,J}\)]
401	  \label{def:gr_transition}
402	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionMap}
403	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, lem:gr_transition_pre_unit, lem:mathlib_away_lift, def:gr_affine_chart}
404	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together

406	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
407	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
408	  % "For any $I$ and $J$, a ring homomorphism
409	  %  $\theta_{I,J} : \Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \to \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$ is
410	  %  defined as follows. ...
411	  %  In particular, we have $\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = 1/P^I_J$,
412	  %  so the map extends to $\Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I]$."
413	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). The away-localisation lift of the
414	  %   pre-hom along `P^J_I`:
415	  %   `transitionMap d r I J :`
416	  %   `Localization.Away (minorDet d r J I) →+* Localization.Away (minorDet d r I J)`
417	  %   `:= IsLocalization.Away.lift (minorDet d r J I)`
418	  %   `(isUnit_transitionPreMap_minorDet d r I J)` applied to
419	  %   `(transitionPreMap d r I J : R^J →+* R^I_J)`.
420	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
421	  Because the pre-localisation hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to
422	  R^I_J\) (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}) sends \(P^J_I\) to a unit of \(R^I_J\)
423	  (\cref{lem:gr_transition_pre_unit}), the universal property of the
424	  away-localisation (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) provides a unique ring
425	  homomorphism extending it along \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to \mathbb{Z}[X^J, 1/P^J_I]\).
426	  This extension is the \emph{transition map}
427	  \[
428	    \theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \longrightarrow
429	    \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J], \qquad \theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I .
430	  \]
431	  Dually \(\theta_{I,J}\) is the comorphism of a morphism of schemes
432	  \(U^I_J \to U^J_I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}), which we also denote
433	  \(\theta_{I,J}\); it sends the universal matrix \(X^I\) to \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\).
434	\end{definition}
435	
436	\begin{lemma}
437	
438	\leanok
439	[\(\theta_{I,I}\) is the identity]
440	  \label{lem:gr_transition_self}
441	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionMap_self}
442	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_minor_det}
443	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
444	  % affine patches" (read from
445	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L838-L848).
446	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
447	  % "Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$".
448	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target).
449	  %   `transitionMap_self d r I : transitionMap d r I I = RingHom.id _`
450	  %   (up to the identification `R^I_I = R^I` from `P^I_I = 1`).
451	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
452	  For every \(I\), \(\theta_{I,I} = \id_{U^I}\): since \(P^I_I = 1\)
453	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}) the minor \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\) is its own
454	  inverse, so \(\theta_{I,I}(X^I) = (X^I_I)^{-1} X^I = X^I\), i.e.\
455	  \(\theta_{I,I}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}) is the identity ring homomorphism
456	  of \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_I] = \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\).
457	\end{lemma}
458	\begin{proof}
459	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_minor_det}
460	  \leanok
461	  When \(J = I\) one has \(P^I_I = \det(1_{d\times d}) = 1\)
462	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}), so \(R^I_I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\), the minor \(X^I_I\)
463	  is the identity, and its Cramer inverse is again the identity. Hence the image
464	  matrix is \(M = 1 \cdot X^I = X^I\), the pre-hom sends each \(x^I_{p,q}\) to
465	  itself, and its localisation lift (\cref{def:gr_transition}) is the identity.
466	\end{proof}

468	\subsection*{Project-local matrix and minor bookkeeping}
469	
470	The cocycle computation rests on a handful of elementary matrix-algebra
471	identities over the (localised) chart rings. They are project-internal helpers
472	with no external source; each is verified directly in Lean.
473	
474	\begin{lemma}
475	
476	\leanok
477	[Column submatrix of a product]
478	  \label{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col}
479	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.mul_submatrix_col}
480	  Let \(A \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times d}(R)\) and
481	  \(B \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times r}(R)\) be matrices over a commutative ring,
482	  and \(g : \{1,\dots,d\} \to \{1,\dots,r\}\) a column-index map. Then taking the
483	  column submatrix of the product equals multiplying by the column submatrix of
484	  the right factor:
485	  \[
486	    (A B)_{\bullet\, g} \;=\; A \,\bigl(B_{\bullet\, g}\bigr).
487	  \]
488	\end{lemma}
489	\begin{proof}
490	
491	\leanok
492	  Entrywise from the definition of matrix multiplication (reindexing the rows by
493	  the identity leaves the contraction index untouched). Proved directly in Lean.
494	\end{proof}
495	
496	\begin{lemma}
497	
498	\leanok
499	[A ring map commutes with the Cramer inverse]
500	  \label{lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv}
501	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.map_nonsing_inv}
502	  \uses{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
503	  Let \(f : R \to S\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and
504	  \(A \in \operatorname{Mat}_{n\times n}(R)\) a square matrix with \(\det A\) a
505	  unit. Writing \(f_\ast\) for the entrywise application of \(f\), the
506	  nonsingular inverse is natural:
507	  \[
508	    \bigl(f_\ast A\bigr)^{-1} \;=\; f_\ast\bigl(A^{-1}\bigr).
509	  \]
510	\end{lemma}
511	\begin{proof}
512	
513	\leanok
514	  Since \(f_\ast A \cdot f_\ast(A^{-1}) = f_\ast(A A^{-1}) = f_\ast(1_{n\times n})
515	  = 1_{n\times n}\) (using \(A A^{-1} = 1\), \cref{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}),
516	  the matrix \(f_\ast(A^{-1})\) is a right inverse of \(f_\ast A\), hence equals
517	  \((f_\ast A)^{-1}\). Proved directly in Lean.
518	\end{proof}
519	
520	\begin{lemma}
521	
522	\leanok
523	[Functoriality of entrywise application]
524	  \label{lem:gr_map_map_eq_of_comp}
525	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.map_map_eq_of_comp}
526	  Let \(f : R \to A\), \(g : A \to D\) and \(h : R \to D\) be ring
527	  homomorphisms with \(g \circ f = h\). Then for any matrix \(M\) over \(R\),
528	  applying \(f\) then \(g\) entrywise equals applying \(h\) entrywise:
529	  \(g_\ast(f_\ast M) = h_\ast M\).
530	\end{lemma}
531	\begin{proof}
532	
533	\leanok
534	  Immediate from functoriality of \(\operatorname{Mat}(-)\) (composition of
535	  entrywise maps). Proved directly in Lean.
536	\end{proof}
537	
538	\begin{lemma}
539	
540	\leanok
541	[Inverse cancellation in a composite]
542	  \label{lem:gr_inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel}
543	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel}
544	  \uses{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
545	  Let \(A, B \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times d}(R)\) and
546	  \(M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times e}(R)\) over a commutative ring, with
547	  \(\det A\) a unit. Then
548	  \[
549	    \bigl(B^{-1} A\bigr)\,\bigl(A^{-1} M\bigr) \;=\; B^{-1} M.
550	  \]
551	\end{lemma}
552	\begin{proof}
553	
554	\leanok
555	  By associativity and \(A A^{-1} = 1_{d\times d}\)
556	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}). Proved directly in Lean.
557	\end{proof}
558	
559	\begin{lemma}
560	
561	\leanok
562	[The \(I\)-minor of \(X^I\) is the identity]
563	  \label{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
564	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
565	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix}
566	  The \(I\)-minor of the universal matrix \(X^I\)
567	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}), with its \(I\)-columns read through the order
568	  isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \xrightarrow{\sim} I\), is the \(d\times d\)
569	  identity: \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\). (This is the normalisation \(P^I_I = 1\)
570	  defining the chart.)
571	\end{lemma}
572	\begin{proof}
573	
574	\leanok
575	  By \cref{def:gr_universal_matrix} the \(q\)-column with \(q \in I\) is the
576	  standard basis vector \(e_p\) where \(p\) is the preimage of \(q\) under the
577	  order isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \xrightarrow{\sim} I\); so the \(I\)-minor
578	  is the identity. Proved directly in Lean.
579	\end{proof}
580	
581	\begin{lemma}
582	
583	\leanok
584	[The \(J\)-minor of the image matrix is the identity]
585	  \label{lem:gr_imageMatrix_submatrix_self}
586	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.imageMatrix_submatrix_self}
587	  \uses{def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}
588	  The \(J\)-minor of the image matrix \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\)
589	  (\cref{def:gr_image_matrix}) is the identity:
590	  \[
591	    M_J \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I_J \;=\; 1_{d\times d}.
592	  \]
593	\end{lemma}
594	\begin{proof}
595	
596	\leanok
597	  The \(J\)-column submatrix of \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) is
598	  \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) times the \(J\)-column submatrix of \(X^I\), i.e.
599	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_J\), by \cref{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col}; this is
600	  \(1_{d\times d}\) by the left-inverse identity
601	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}). Proved directly in Lean.
602	\end{proof}
603	
604	\begin{lemma}
605	
606	\leanok
607	[The \(I\)-minor of the image matrix is the Cramer inverse]
608	  \label{lem:gr_imageMatrix_submatrix_I}
609	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.imageMatrix_submatrix_I}
610	  \uses{def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col, lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
611	  The \(I\)-minor of the image matrix \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\)
612	  (\cref{def:gr_image_matrix}) is the Cramer inverse:
613	  \[
614	    M_I \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I_I \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1}.
615	  \]
616	\end{lemma}
617	\begin{proof}
618	
619	\leanok
620	  By \cref{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col} the \(I\)-column submatrix of \(M\) is
621	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I\), and \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\)
622	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}). Proved directly in Lean.
623	\end{proof}
624	
625	\begin{lemma}
626	
627	\leanok
628	[\(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) realises the matrix formula]
629	  \label{lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap}
630	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap}
631	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_image_matrix, def:gr_transition_pre, lem:gr_imageMatrix_submatrix_self}
632	  Applying the pre-localisation hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\)
633	  (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}) entrywise to the universal matrix \(X^J\) yields
634	  the image matrix:
635	  \[
636	    (\tilde\theta_{I,J})_\ast\, X^J \;=\; M \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I .
637	  \]
638	\end{lemma}
639	\begin{proof}
640	
641	\leanok
642	  On a free entry \(x^J_{p,q}\) (\(q \notin J\)) this holds by the definition of
643	  \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) as evaluation at the entries of \(M\)
644	  (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}). On a column \(q \in J\) the entry of \(X^J\) is
645	  an identity-block entry, which \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) carries to the
646	  corresponding entry of \(M_J = 1_{d\times d}\)
647	  (\cref{lem:gr_imageMatrix_submatrix_self}). Proved directly in Lean.
648	\end{proof}
649	
650	\begin{lemma}
651	
652	\leanok
653	[Image of a cross minor under \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\)]
654	  \label{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet}
655	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionPreMap_minorDet}
656	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, def:gr_minor_det, def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap}
657	  For a third size-\(d\) subset \(K\), the pre-hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) sends the
658	  minor determinant \(P^J_K\) (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}) to the determinant of the
659	  \(K\)-minor of the image matrix \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\):
660	  \[
661	    \tilde\theta_{I,J}\bigl(P^J_K\bigr) \;=\; \det\bigl(M_K\bigr).
662	  \]
663	\end{lemma}
664	\begin{proof}
665	
666	\leanok
667	  The hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) commutes with determinants and with passing to
668	  a column submatrix, so \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_K) =
669	  \det\bigl((\tilde\theta_{I,J})_\ast X^J\bigr)_K\); apply
670	  \cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap}. Proved directly in Lean.
671	\end{proof}
672	
673	\begin{lemma}
674	
675	\leanok
676	[Naturality of the image matrix]
677	  \label{lem:gr_imageMatrix_map_eq}
678	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.imageMatrix_map_eq}
679	  \uses{def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_map_map_eq_of_comp, def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv, lem:gr_minorDet_unit, def:gr_universal_minor}
680	  Let \(X\) be a size-\(d\) subset and \(\iota : R^I_X \to D\) a ring
681	  homomorphism lying over the structure map \(R^I \to D\), i.e.\
682	  \(\iota \circ (R^I \to R^I_X) = (R^I \to D)\). Writing \(Y\) for the universal
683	  matrix \(X^I\) base-changed to \(D\) and \(Y_X\) for its \(X\)-minor, one has
684	  \[
685	    \iota_\ast\bigl((X^I_X)^{-1} X^I\bigr) \;=\; (Y_X)^{-1}\, Y .
686	  \]
687	\end{lemma}
688	\begin{proof}
689	
690	\leanok
691	  Distribute \(\iota_\ast\) across the product \(M = (X^I_X)^{-1} X^I\); the
692	  factor \((X^I_X)^{-1}\) passes through \(\iota_\ast\) as a Cramer inverse
693	  (\cref{lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv}, using \cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}), and both
694	  factors collapse to base changes over \(D\) by functoriality
695	  (\cref{lem:gr_map_map_eq_of_comp}) since \(\iota\) lies over \(R^I \to D\).
696	  Proved directly in Lean.
697	\end{proof}
698	
699	\subsection*{Triple-overlap rings and the localised transition maps}
700	
701	The cocycle condition \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\) cannot
702	be stated as a naive composition of the landed transition maps of
703	\cref{def:gr_transition}: the codomain of \(\theta_{J,K}\) (the ring
704	\(R^J[1/P^J_K]\)) differs from the domain of \(\theta_{I,J}\) (the ring
705	\(R^J[1/P^J_I]\)). The identity therefore lives over the \emph{triple-overlap}
706	rings obtained by inverting both relevant minors in each chart ring:
707	\[
708	  S_K := R^K[1/(P^K_I P^K_J)], \quad
709	  S_J := R^J[1/(P^J_I P^J_K)], \quad
710	  S_I := R^I[1/(P^I_J P^I_K)].
711	\]
712	
713	\begin{definition}
714	
715	\leanok
716	[Inclusion of an away-localisation into a double one (left)]
717	  \label{def:gr_away_incl_left}
718	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayInclLeft}
719	  \uses{lem:mathlib_away_lift, lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
720	  For \(x, y \in R\), the canonical inclusion
721	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}}_{x,y} : R[1/x] \to R[1/(xy)]\) inverting the extra factor
722	  \(y\), defined as the away-localisation lift (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) of
723	  the structure map \(R \to R[1/(xy)]\) along \(x\) — well-defined because \(x\)
724	  is a unit of \(R[1/(xy)]\) (a factor of the unit \(xy\),
725	  \cref{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}).
726	\end{definition}
727	
728	\begin{definition}
729	
730	\leanok
731	[Inclusion of an away-localisation into a double one (right)]
732	  \label{def:gr_away_incl_right}
733	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayInclRight}
734	  \uses{lem:mathlib_away_lift, lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
735	  For \(x, y \in R\), the canonical inclusion
736	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{R}}_{x,y} : R[1/y] \to R[1/(xy)]\) inverting the extra factor
737	  \(x\), defined as the away-localisation lift (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) of
738	  \(R \to R[1/(xy)]\) along \(y\) — well-defined since \(y\) is a unit of
739	  \(R[1/(xy)]\) (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}).
740	\end{definition}
741	
742	\begin{lemma}
743	
744	\leanok
745	[Left inclusion lies over \(R\)]
746	  \label{lem:gr_awayInclLeft_comp_algebraMap}
747	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayInclLeft_comp_algebraMap}
748	  \uses{def:gr_away_incl_left}
749	  The inclusion \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}}_{x,y}\) (\cref{def:gr_away_incl_left})
750	  intertwines the two structure maps:
751	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}}_{x,y} \circ (R \to R[1/x]) = (R \to R[1/(xy)])\).
752	\end{lemma}
753	\begin{proof}
754	
755	\leanok
756	  By the defining property of the away-localisation lift
757	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}). Proved directly in Lean.
758	\end{proof}
759	
760	\begin{lemma}
761	
762	\leanok
763	[Right inclusion lies over \(R\)]
764	  \label{lem:gr_awayInclRight_comp_algebraMap}
765	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayInclRight_comp_algebraMap}
766	  \uses{def:gr_away_incl_right}
767	  The inclusion \(\iota^{\mathrm{R}}_{x,y}\) (\cref{def:gr_away_incl_right})
768	  intertwines the two structure maps:
769	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{R}}_{x,y} \circ (R \to R[1/y]) = (R \to R[1/(xy)])\).
770	\end{lemma}
771	\begin{proof}
772	
773	\leanok
774	  By the defining property of the away-localisation lift
775	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}). Proved directly in Lean.
776	\end{proof}
777	
778	\begin{lemma}
779	
780	\leanok
781	[Left factor is a unit in the double localisation]
782	  \label{lem:gr_isUnit_algebraMap_away_left}
783	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isUnit_algebraMap_away_left}
784	  \uses{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
785	  For \(x, y \in R\), the image of \(x\) in \(R[1/(xy)]\) is a unit.
786	\end{lemma}
787	\begin{proof}
788	
789	\leanok
790	  \(xy\) is a unit of \(R[1/(xy)]\)
791	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}); a factor of a unit is a unit.
792	  Proved directly in Lean.
793	\end{proof}
794	
795	\begin{lemma}
796	
797	\leanok
798	[Right factor is a unit in the double localisation]
799	  \label{lem:gr_isUnit_algebraMap_away_right}
800	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isUnit_algebraMap_away_right}
801	  \uses{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
802	  For \(x, y \in R\), the image of \(y\) in \(R[1/(xy)]\) is a unit.
803	\end{lemma}
804	\begin{proof}
805	
806	\leanok
807	  \(xy\) is a unit of \(R[1/(xy)]\)
808	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}); a factor of a unit is a unit.
809	  Proved directly in Lean.
810	\end{proof}
811	
812	\begin{lemma}
813	
814	\leanok
815	[The cross minor becomes a unit after inclusion]
816	  \label{lem:gr_isUnit_incl_transitionPreMap_cross}
817	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isUnit_incl_transitionPreMap_cross}
818	  \uses{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, def:gr_image_matrix}
819	  Let \(A, B, C\) be size-\(d\) subsets and \(\iota : R^A_B \to D\) a ring
820	  homomorphism over the structure map \(R^A \to D\) under which the minor
821	  determinant \(P^A_C\) becomes a unit of \(D\). Then
822	  \(\iota\bigl(\tilde\theta_{A,B}(P^B_C)\bigr)\) is a unit of \(D\). Indeed
823	  \[
824	    \tilde\theta_{A,B}\bigl(P^B_C\bigr)
825	      = \det\bigl((X^A_B)^{-1} X^A_C\bigr)
826	      = \det\bigl((X^A_B)^{-1}\bigr)\cdot P^A_C ,
827	  \]
828	  a product of two units once \(P^A_C\) is inverted in \(D\).
829	\end{lemma}
830	\begin{proof}
831	
832	\leanok
833	  By \cref{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet},
834	  \(\tilde\theta_{A,B}(P^B_C) = \det(M_C)\) with \(M = (X^A_B)^{-1} X^A\); split
835	  the \(C\)-minor of the product (\cref{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col}) and the
836	  determinant as \(\det((X^A_B)^{-1}) \cdot P^A_C\). The first factor is a unit
837	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}); the second becomes a unit under
838	  \(\iota\) by hypothesis. Proved directly in Lean.
839	\end{proof}
840	
841	\begin{definition}
842

994	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$, and
995	% we leave it to the reader to verify that
996	% for any three subsets $I$, $J$ and $K$ of $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of
997	% cardinality $d$, the co-cycle condition
998	% $\theta_{I,K}= \theta_{I,J}\theta_{J,K}$

1000	% L838-L848.)
1001	\begin{proof}
1002	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_transition_self, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_cocycle_theta_jk, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ik, lem:gr_cocycle_imageMatrix_eq}
1003	  \leanok
1004	  We verify the scheme-morphism form
1005	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) by a direct matrix
1006	  computation, valid over the open locus of \(U^I\) on which all the minors
1007	  involved are invertible (namely \(P^I_J\) and \(P^I_K\), which suffices since
1008	  the maps are determined by their restriction to a dense open and all rings are
1009	  domains; the computation is an identity of matrices with entries in
1010	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J, 1/P^I_K]\)).
1011	
1012	  Start from the matrix \(X^I\) (the universal point of \(U^I\)). By
1013	  \cref{def:gr_transition},
1014	  \[
1015	    M^J \;:=\; \theta_{I,J}(X^I) \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I,
1016	  \]
1017	  a \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(J\)-minor is the identity, hence a point of
1018	  \(U^J\). Its \(K\)-minor is
1019	  \[
1020	    M^J_K \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_K,
1021	    \qquad\text{so}\qquad
1022	    (M^J_K)^{-1} \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I_J,
1023	  \]
1024	  using \((AB)^{-1} = B^{-1} A^{-1}\) for the invertible \(d \times d\) matrices
1025	  \(X^I_J\) and \(X^I_K\). Applying \(\theta_{J,K}\) to \(M^J\) gives
1026	  \[
1027	    \theta_{J,K}(M^J) \;=\; (M^J_K)^{-1} M^J
1028	    \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I_J \,\cdot\, (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I
1029	    \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I,
1030	  \]
1031	  the middle product \(X^I_J (X^I_J)^{-1} = 1_{d\times d}\) cancelling by
1032	  associativity of matrix multiplication. The right-hand side is precisely
1033	  \(\theta_{I,K}(X^I) = (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I\). Thus
1034	  \(\theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) and \(\theta_{I,K}\) agree on the
1035	  universal matrix \(X^I\), hence agree as morphisms \(U^I \supseteq U^I_J \cap
1036	  U^I_K \to U^K\). The special case \(K = I\) (resp.\ \(J = I\)) together with
1037	  \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\) shows each \(\theta_{I,J}\) is an isomorphism onto its
1038	  image with inverse \(\theta_{J,I}\), since
1039	  \(\theta_{J,I} \circ \theta_{I,J} = \theta_{I,I} = \id\).
1040	\end{proof}

971	\begin{lemma}[Pullback of a pair of affine \(\operatorname{Spec}\)-maps]

977	  induced morphisms \(\operatorname{Spec}(S) \to \operatorname{Spec}(R)\) and
978	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(T) \to \operatorname{Spec}(R)\) is canonically the spectrum of

984	    \qquad (\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.pullbackSpecIso}),

996	\begin{lemma}[Cancellation of base change for the tensor tower]
997	  \label{lem:cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
998	  \lean{TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.cancelBaseChange}
999	  \mathlibok
1000	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1001	  Let \(R \to A \to B\) be a tower of algebras, \(M\) a \(B\)-module that is also an
1002	  \(A\)- and \(R\)-module compatibly, and \(N\) an \(R\)-module. Then there is a
1003	  \(B\)-linear equivalence, carrying \emph{no} flatness hypothesis,
1004	  \[
1005	    \operatorname{cancelBaseChange} :
1006	    M \otimes_A (A \otimes_R N) \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; M \otimes_R N,
1007	  \]
1008	  with \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\bigl(m \otimes_A (a \otimes_R n)\bigr)
1009	  = (a \cdot m) \otimes_R n\) (\texttt{cancelBaseChange\_tmul}) and inverse
1010	  \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}^{-1}(m \otimes_R n) = m \otimes_A (1 \otimes_R n)\)
1011	  (\texttt{cancelBaseChange\_symm\_tmul}). Instantiated at the affine--affine model
1012	  (\(M = R'\), \(B = R'\), \(N = M\)) this is the cancellation isomorphism
1013	  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M \cong R' \otimes_R M\) used below, read in either
1014	  orientation.

1088	  change'' (proof, ``describe pullbacks and pushforwards'' step).}

1092	  base-change map are canonically \(R' \otimes_R M\) as an \(R'\)-module. Explicitly,
1093	  the assembled isomorphism

1097	  \]

1102	  extension of scalars \(R' \otimes_R (-)\) along \(\psi\)).

1236	% --- Supporting lemmas for the codomain read ---
1237	% These helpers establish that pullback along a scheme isomorphism is an equivalence
1238	% of module categories; the adjunction unit of an equivalence is an isomorphism,
1239	% used in the codomain-read computation.

1241	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1242	[Pullback along an isomorphism is an equivalence of module categories]
1243	  \label{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1244	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1245	  Let \(f : X \to Y\) be an isomorphism of schemes. Then the pullback functor on
1246	  \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules \(f^* : Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \to X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\) is
1247	  one half of an equivalence of categories
1248	  \(Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \simeq X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\), with quasi-inverse
1249	  \((f^{-1})^*\).
1250	\end{lemma}
1251	\begin{proof}
1252	
1253	  Assemble the equivalence from the functor \(f^*\) and the candidate inverse
1254	  \((f^{-1})^*\). The unit and counit isomorphisms are built from the pseudofunctor
1255	  coherences of pullback: \((f^{-1})^* \circ f^* \cong (f \circ f^{-1})^* = \mathrm{id}^*
1256	  \cong \mathrm{id}\) and symmetrically, using
1257	  \(f \circ f^{-1} = \mathrm{id}_Y\), \(f^{-1} \circ f = \mathrm{id}_X\)
1258	  (composition-of-pullbacks, congruence under equal maps, and pullback-of-identity).
1259	  The triangle identities are forced by these coherences.
1260	\end{proof}
1261	
1262	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1263	[The pullback functor along an isomorphism is an equivalence]
1264	  \label{lem:pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso}
1265	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso}
1266	  \uses{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1267	  Consequently, for an isomorphism \(f : X \to Y\) the functor
1268	  \(f^* : Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \to X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\) is an equivalence of
1269	  categories. In particular its adjunction unit is a natural isomorphism, so
1270	  \((\eta_{f})_{W}\) is invertible on every object \(W\).
1271	\end{lemma}
1272	\begin{proof}
1273	
1274	  The functor underlying the equivalence of
1275	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso} is exactly \(f^*\), so \(f^*\) is an
1276	  equivalence; an equivalence's unit is a natural isomorphism.
1277	\end{proof}

1283	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1284	[Regrouping isomorphism for the section-level mate]
1285	  \label{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}
1286	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}
1287	  % NOTE: tensor-order convention. The Lean uses Mathlib's `pullbackSpecIso`
1288	  % orientation `A ⊗[R] R'` (so the codomain is `(A ⊗_R R') ⊗_A M` and the generator
1289	  % goes `r' ⊗ m ↦ (1 ⊗ r') ⊗ m`); the prose below writes `R' ⊗_R A`. The two are the
1290	  % same module up to `TensorProduct.comm` — a faithful re-orientation, no content change.
1291	  \uses{lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv, lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
1292	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
1293	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
1294	  % "boils down to the equality" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
1295	  % L933--938).
1296	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
1297	  % equality
1298	  % $$
1299	  % (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
1300	  % $$
1301	  % as $R'$-modules."
1302	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
1303	  change'' (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
1304	  Let \(\psi : R \to R'\) and \(\varphi : R \to A\) be ring homomorphisms and \(M\) an
1305	  \(A\)-module. There is a bundled \(R'\)-linear isomorphism
1306	  \[
1307	    \operatorname{regroup} :
1308	    (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M ,
1309	  \]
1310	  the ``equality as \(R'\)-modules'' of the source, sending the generator
1311	  \((r' \otimes 1) \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes m\); equivalently its inverse sends
1312	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m\). It carries \emph{no} flatness
1313	  hypothesis.
1314	\end{lemma}
1315	
1316	\begin{proof}
1317	  \uses{lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv, lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
1318	  The underlying \(R'\)-linear equivalence is supplied, already as a bundled
1319	  \(\operatorname{LinearEquiv}\) over \(R'\), by the standalone geometry-free helper
1320	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}, whose construction is now the
1321	  natively \(R'\)-linear pushout-cancellation equivalence of
1322	  Lemma~\ref{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}. Crucially, that helper bundles
1323	  \(R'\)-linearity \emph{internally}: there is no hand-written scalar-compatibility
1324	  obligation, no induction over elementary tensors, and in particular \emph{no} residual
1325	  \(r' \cdot 0 = 0\) bookkeeping branch. The only work left to this proof is therefore to
1326	  promote that \(R'\)-linear equivalence to a morphism of \(R'\)-module \emph{objects} in
1327	  the exact extension/restriction-of-scalars packaging that the codomain and domain reads
1328	  produce. We work in the \(A \otimes_R R'\) orientation
1329	  (the same module as \(R' \otimes_R A\) up to the canonical commutativity isomorphism,
1330	  per the tensor-order convention note).
1331	
1332	  \emph{The lone residual obstruction is the \(A\)-action diamond, resolved at the object
1333	  level.} The pushout-cancellation helper takes its relative tensor product over the
1334	  \emph{canonical} \(A\)-algebra structure on \(A \otimes_R R'\)
1335	  (multiplication in the left factor), whereas the carrier object produced by the
1336	  dictionaries,
1337	  the extension of scalars of \(M\) along the left inclusion \(\iota : A \to A \otimes_R R'\),
1338	  tensors over the \(A\)-module structure on \(A \otimes_R R'\) induced by \(\iota\). These
1339	  two \(A\)-module structures on
1340	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) agree on elements but are distinct as data, so the two relative
1341	  tensor products are distinct types even though their underlying abelian groups and
1342	  their \(R'\)-actions coincide. This diamond is the \emph{only} thing the helper does
1343	  not already discharge, and — because the \(R'\)-linearity is now entirely internal to
1344	  the Mathlib equivalence — it is resolved purely at the carrier level by an
1345	  identity-on-elements \(R'\)-linear bridge
1346	  \[
1347	    eT : (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A^{\,\operatorname{restr}} M
1348	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1349	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A^{\,\mathrm{can}} M ,
1350	  \]
1351	  the identity map on generators, which is \(R'\)-linear because both sides carry the
1352	  same \(R'\)-action (restriction of scalars along \(R' \to A \otimes_R R'\)).
1353	
1354	  Composing the bridge \(eT\) with the natively \(R'\)-linear helper equivalence and
1355	  promoting to an isomorphism of module objects yields the desired bundled
1356	  \(R'\)-linear isomorphism of \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(R')\) objects. Being a composite
1357	  of linear equivalences it is an \(R'\)-linear isomorphism, and none of its constituents
1358	  carries a flatness hypothesis.
1359	\end{proof}

1434	\begin{lemma}[Unit-across-conjugate coherence]
1435	  \label{lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_mathlib}
